mechanism: Decision-makers select among information items. When information quantity is low (2 items), selection criterion is information direction (consistent/inconsistent), activating desire for appearance of unbiasedness, causing preference for inconsistent information. When quantity is high (10+ items), direction alone cannot discriminate, so selection switches to expected information quality assessment, biased toward consistent items, causing consistent-item preference. Executive control resolves criterion-based goal conflict.

Self-regulation resource depletion state primes motivational avoidance of inconsistent information. Depleted individuals show increased commitment to held standpoint, which triggers stronger dissonance-based preference for consistent information. Executive control is weakened, so defensive goal-driven information filtering dominates. Ego threat and cognitive load produce opposite effects on consistency-preference because they lack the prior motivational priming of depletion.

Goal-pursuit phase (implemental vs. deliberative) activates distinct cognitive mind-sets via procedural priming. Implemental phase encodes one-sided information processing and confidence-boosting orientation that spreads to unrelated attitude domains. Attitudes activate closed-minded evaluative focus in implemental state, manifesting as stronger attitudes (higher extremity, lower ambivalence, faster accessibility) toward unrelated objects. Mind-set carryover transfers procedural filters across cognitive domains.

Interpersonal risk (threat of rejection/dependence) automatically activates dual competing goals: connectedness (approach partner) and self-protection (avoid partner). Executive control system arbitrates goal conflict by appraising partner acceptance likelihood and signaling acceptance/rejection feedback. High-self-esteem individuals' control system prioritizes connectedness goals; low-self-esteem individuals' control system suppresses connectedness execution despite goal activation. Goal conflict intensity modulates with risk immediacy and goal strength.

Perceived personal capability for similar transgression mediates forgiveness through three channels: reduced offense severity perception, enhanced empathic understanding, and increased self-offender similarity recognition. Each pathway independently weakens retribution motivation and strengthens benevolent appraisal. Severity reduction activates cost-benefit reappraisal; empathic understanding activates perspective-taking; similarity activates humility-driven egalitarianism. Gender and offense-similarity conditions moderate pathway strength.

Recognition task on visual nonverbal expression. Pride expression (head-tilt-back, expanded posture, smile) activates categorical classification. Target characteristics (ethnicity, gender) influence classification accuracy through status-based expectancy bias and cultural stereotype associations. Perceiver cultural background modulates baseline recognition via exposure to Western expression variants. Cross-cultural transmission risk affects whether expression is culture-universal or culture-specific learned gesture.
